skill.name: citation-management
skill.description: Search academic sources, extract citation metadata, validate references, and format BibTeX bibliographies for research writing.
skill.summary: Manage academic citations with PubMed, Google Scholar, DOI metadata, and BibTeX validation tools.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found no confirmed malicious intent, prompt injection, hidden command execution, or obfuscated payloads. The real risks are expected for this skill: external academic API calls, optional NCBI credential use, optional Google Scholar proxying, and local file writes. Publish with a network, credential, and file-overwrite warning.

medium_findings.title: Expected External Academic API Access
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for network access, but legitimate for this skill. The scripts call DOI, CrossRef, PubMed, arXiv, and Google Scholar services to retrieve citation metadata. This can expose search terms and identifiers to those services, so users need a network-access warning rather than a block.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct HTTP client usage and hardcoded academic API URLs were confirmed in executable scripts. The behavior matches the documented citation workflow, so risk is privacy and dependency exposure, not malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: NCBI Credential Environment Access
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for environment access. PubMed-related scripts read NCBI_API_KEY and NCBI_EMAIL and include them in NCBI API parameters. This is a normal E-utilities pattern, but it handles user credentials and should be disclosed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable names and API parameter use are explicit. I found no evidence that credentials are sent anywhere except the NCBI E-utilities workflow.

medium_findings.title: Optional Third-Party Proxy for Google Scholar
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for a privacy-sensitive option. The Google Scholar script can enable free proxies through the scholarly library. This may route research queries through third-party infrastructure, even though it is opt-in and intended to avoid rate limiting.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly creates a ProxyGenerator and enables FreeProxies when requested. The exact proxy provider is controlled by the scholarly library, so the privacy impact is clear but contextual.

low_findings.title: User-Controlled File Writes and In-Place Formatting
low_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for filesystem writes. The scripts write generated citations, reports, or formatted BibTeX to user-selected paths, and the formatter overwrites the input file when no output path is supplied. This is expected CLI behavior but can overwrite local files if used carelessly.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write calls are direct and visible. They use paths supplied by command-line arguments or the input file, which makes the behavior user-directed rather than hidden.

low_findings.title: Static Command, Crypto, and Obfuscation Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for most external command, weak cryptography, and obfuscation alerts. The backtick detections occur in Markdown command examples and BibTeX documentation, not Ruby execution. The weak crypto and certificate/key alerts are caused by citation words such as key and BibTeX syntax. No evidence found of eval, subprocess, os.system, encoded payloads, or prompt-injection override text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found documentation examples and ordinary Python data structures at representative flagged locations. Because the static list is very large, this is a category-level evaluation rather than line-by-line confirmation of every duplicate alert.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Require User Judgment
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for automatic execution, with minor operational risk. The skill documentation includes many shell examples for running local Python scripts and installing dependencies. They are instructions for the user, not hidden command execution, but users should review commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious syntax is located in Markdown examples and setup guidance. The executable scripts reviewed do not contain shell-spawning APIs, but the documentation still asks users to run commands manually.

content.value_statement: Research projects need accurate citations across many sources. This skill searches academic databases, extracts metadata, validates references, and formats BibTeX entries.
actual_capabilities: Search PubMed and export citation metadata as JSON or BibTeX.
Search Google Scholar through the scholarly library with optional year and citation sorting.
Convert DOI, PMID, arXiv ID, or article URL inputs into BibTeX entries.
Clean, sort, deduplicate, and reformat existing BibTeX files.
Validate BibTeX fields, duplicate entries, DOI formats, and optional DOI resolution.
Process single identifiers or batch input files for bibliography workflows.
limitations: Google Scholar search depends on the third-party scholarly library and may be rate limited.
Metadata quality depends on external services such as CrossRef, PubMed, arXiv, and DataCite.
The BibTeX parser handles common entries but may miss complex nested braces or macros.
Network features can expose search queries and identifiers to academic APIs.

use_cases.title: Build a Manuscript Bibliography
use_cases.description: Collect DOI and PMID identifiers, convert them to BibTeX, and validate required fields before submission.

use_cases.title: Audit Reference Accuracy
use_cases.description: Check a BibTeX file for missing fields, duplicate entries, invalid DOI formats, and inconsistent author formatting.

use_cases.title: Find Biomedical Literature
use_cases.description: Search PubMed with date filters and publication types, then export relevant results for screening.

prompt_templates.title: Convert One DOI
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this DOI into a clean BibTeX citation and explain any missing metadata: 10.1038/s41586-021-03819-2

prompt_templates.title: Validate a Bibliography
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate my BibTeX file for missing required fields, duplicate entries, DOI problems, and inconsistent author formatting.

prompt_templates.title: Search PubMed With Filters
prompt_templates.prompt: Search PubMed for recent clinical trials on Alzheimer disease drug therapy from 2020 to 2024 and export candidate citations.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Citation Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a repeatable workflow to collect papers, extract metadata, deduplicate references, validate DOIs, and produce a final BibTeX library.

output_examples.input: A DOI for a Nature article
output_examples.output: A formatted BibTeX citation with title, authors, journal, year, DOI, and a generated citation key.

output_examples.input: A BibTeX file with repeated entries and missing fields
output: Missing required fields listed by entry
Suggested formatting fixes for pages and authors

output_examples.input: A PubMed query with date and publication type filters
output_examples.output: A ranked set of retrieved papers with PMIDs, titles, authors, journals, years, abstracts, and optional BibTeX export.

best_practices: Review generated metadata against publisher or database records before submission.
Use API keys only for the intended academic services and avoid sharing them in prompts.
Write outputs to a separate file before replacing an important bibliography.
anti_patterns: Do not assume Google Scholar results are complete or stable across repeated searches.
Do not run DOI validation on large files without considering external API rate limits.
Do not trust automatically generated citation keys without checking for naming collisions.

faq.question: Does this skill require network access?
faq.answer: Yes. Search, DOI conversion, metadata extraction, and DOI verification call external academic services.

faq.question: Can it work without an NCBI API key?
faq.answer: Yes. PubMed features can run without a key, but NCBI allows higher request rates when a key is provided.

faq.question: Does it execute shell commands automatically?
faq.answer: No evidence found of automatic shell command execution in the scripts. The Markdown files contain command examples for users.

faq.question: Can it overwrite files?
faq.answer: Yes. Several scripts write to user-selected output paths, and the formatter can overwrite the input file by default.

faq.question: Does it support non-biomedical papers?
faq.answer: Yes. CrossRef, DOI, arXiv, Google Scholar, and BibTeX formatting features support broader academic literature.

faq.question: Should I use the Google Scholar proxy option?
faq.answer: Use it only when acceptable for your privacy policy, because queries may pass through third-party proxy infrastructure.
